questions.stem: A child with fever is breathing faster than usual and has nasal flaring. What should the nurse do first?

options.text: Reassess respiratory status and oxygenation immediately
Tell the parent the child is probably just warm
Wait until the next temperature check
Focus only on giving antipyretics

questions.rationale: Nasal flaring and tachypnea suggest respiratory distress and require prompt assessment. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Fever does not explain away respiratory distress. - C Delay is unsafe. - D Lowering the fever does not replace breathing assessment.

distractorRationales.b: The problem with Tell the parent the child is probably just warm is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, while the decisive cue is Nasal flaring and tachypnea suggest respiratory distress and require prompt assessment. The safer answer is Reassess respiratory status and oxygenation immediately because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: This option makes Wait until the next temperature check the focus, and misses that - D Lowering the fever does not replace breathing assessment. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so It is less defensible because Reassess respiratory status and oxygenation immediately targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Focus only on giving antipyretics is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, without accounting for **Why the others are wrong:** - B Fever does not explain away respiratory distress. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for airway and breathing intervention while oxygenation or ventilation progresses.

questions.takeaway: Nasal flaring is a pediatric respiratory warning sign.

questions.stem: A patient suddenly has facial droop and one-sided arm weakness. What is the nurse's priority?

options.text: Activate the stroke response process immediately
Recheck in an hour to see whether it improves
Ask the patient to rest and avoid talking
Give pain medication first

questions.rationale: Sudden focal deficits can signal stroke, and rapid response matters because time-sensitive treatment may be available. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Waiting can cost treatment time. - C Rest does not address the emergency. - D Pain control is not the first priority here.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor pulls attention toward Recheck in an hour to see whether it improves, even though the higher-risk finding is - D Pain control is not the first priority here. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Activate the stroke response process immediately better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with Ask the patient to rest and avoid talking is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, instead of responding to A patient suddenly has facial droop and one-sided arm weakness. The priority is Activate the stroke response process immediately, not a step that delays stabilization of neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: The reasoning trap in Give pain medication first is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, when the bedside priority is driven by Sudden focal deficits can signal stroke. Activate the stroke response process immediately better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: Sudden one-sided weakness is a stroke emergency until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: A nurse receives report on four patients. Which patient is UNSTABLE and requires immediate reassessment?

options.text: Post-op appendectomy patient, day 1, HR 92, BP 118/74, mild incisional pain 4/10
Patient with pneumonia, T 38.4°C, HR 88, RR 20, SpO₂ 95% on 3L NC, improving on antibiotics
Patient with GI bleed admitted 6 hours ago: HR 118 (was 88 one hour ago), BP 96/58 (was 118/72), increased bright red rectal bleeding
Patient with COPD exacerbation, SpO₂ 91% on 2L NC (baseline 90–92%), pursed-lip breathing

questions.rationale: The GI bleed patient shows a deteriorating trend over one hour: HR increased 30 bpm (compensatory tachycardia from volume loss), BP dropped 22/14 mmHg (hypoperfusion), and bleeding is visibly worsening. This trajectory — not just the absolute values — defines instability. A patient is unstable when vital signs are trending in the wrong direction, indicating the body's compensatory mechanisms are being overwhelmed. The post-op patient (A) is stable and expected. The pneumonia patient (B) is improving on treatment. The COPD patient (D) is at their baseline SpO₂.

distractorRationales.a: Post-op appendectomy patient, day 1, HR 92, BP 118/74, mild incisional pain 4/10 fits a lower-risk interpretation, although the nurse should be guided by The COPD patient (D) is at their baseline SpO₂. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of bleeding or anticoagulation injury and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.b: This answer choice treats Patient with pneumonia, T 38.4°C, HR 88, RR 20, SpO₂ 95% on 3L NC, improving on as the key issue, while the decisive cue is bleeding is visibly worsening. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so The nurse should prioritize Patient with GI bleed admitted 6 hours ago: HR 118 (was 88 one hour ago), BP; otherwise bleeding or anticoagulation injury remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on Patient with COPD exacerbation, SpO₂ 91% on 2L NC (baseline 90–92%), pursed-lip breathing, while the decisive cue is bleeding is visibly worsening. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The nurse should prioritize Patient with GI bleed admitted 6 hours ago: HR 118 (was 88 one hour ago), BP; otherwise bleeding or anticoagulation injury remains the active threat.

questions.takeaway: Instability = trending in the wrong direction. HR rising + BP falling + worsening bleeding = active hemorrhagic shock. Always compare current vitals to the baseline/trend, not just absolute values.

questions.stem: A patient with asthma is wheezing loudly, using accessory muscles, and can only say a few words at a time. What is the best interpretation?

options.text: The patient is having significant respiratory distress
The wheezing proves the airway is opening well
The patient should rest without reassessment
Talking less always means improvement

questions.rationale: Accessory muscle use and limited speech suggest increased work of breathing and a worsening respiratory pattern. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Loud wheezing does not mean the patient is stable. - C Rest does not replace reassessment. - D Short speech can indicate more, not less, distress.

distractorRationales.b: Selecting The wheezing proves the airway is opening well would be unsafe, instead of responding to Speech limitation plus accessory muscle use is a respiratory warning sign. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize The patient is having significant respiratory distress; otherwise oxygenation or ventilation remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on The patient should rest without reassessment, instead of responding to Speech limitation plus accessory muscle use is a respiratory warning sign. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Talking less always means improvement is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, and misses that Accessory muscle use and limited speech suggest increased work of breathing and a worsening respiratory pattern. The correct path is The patient is having significant respiratory distress, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: Speech limitation plus accessory muscle use is a respiratory warning sign.

questions.stem: The nurse has four patients. Which should be seen first?

options.text: A patient with shortness of breath and accessory muscle use
A patient requesting help to the bathroom
A patient asking for a TV remote
A patient waiting for routine teaching

questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor pulls attention toward This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue, without accounting for Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation is safer because it addresses clinical safety risk before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home is that it moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, instead of responding to Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma. The nurse should prioritize The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation; otherwise clinical safety risk remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.d: This option overweights These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well, while the decisive cue is Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay bedside safety action and leave clinical safety risk unresolved.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: The clinical error in Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight is that it moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, even though the higher-risk finding is The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. It is less defensible because Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn, and misses that sodium 128 mEq/L. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: This option overweights Teach the client about low-sodium foods, despite the clinical warning in A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg. Moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, so The safer answer is Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids because delaying renal protection can worsen renal injury.

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: The problem with Obtain a full allergy history before intervening is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, rather than acting on A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives. The priority is Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, instead of responding to A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives. The priority is Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Document the event and continue monitoring is not the best answer, even though the higher-risk finding is The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so It is less defensible because Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: The problem with Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, rather than acting on The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Choosing it can delay neurologic safety assessment and leave neurologic deterioration unresolved.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes, even though the higher-risk finding is Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes, although the nurse should be guided by A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of neurologic deterioration and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.

questions.stem: A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Fifteen minutes later the client reports chills, low back pain, and chest tightness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline.
Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes.
Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion.
Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response.

questions.rationale: Chills, low back pain, and chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. The nurse should stop the blood immediately to prevent further exposure and maintain IV access with normal saline using new tubing so emergency treatment can be given. Slowing the infusion or masking symptoms delays the response to a potentially life-threatening complication.

distractorRationales.b: This option makes Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes the focus, and misses that Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: This option overweights Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion, instead of responding to Chills, low back pain. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of circulation or perfusion and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response is not the best answer, without accounting for chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so The correct path is Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: Selecting Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster would be unsafe, while the decisive cue is c diff spore precautions infection. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so The nurse should prioritize Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves; otherwise infection progression remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: This option makes Place the client on droplet precautions only the focus, without accounting for c diff spore precautions infection. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: This answer choice treats Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel as the key issue, while the decisive cue is c diff spore precautions infection. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so The priority is Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves, not a step that delays stabilization of infection progression.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.

questions.stem: Which action by the nurse best helps prevent a central line-associated bloodstream infection in a hospitalized client?

options.text: Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing.
Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort.
Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures.
Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions.

questions.rationale: Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Using the line casually or leaving it uncapped increases contamination risk. Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone.

distractorRationales.b: Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort would not address the priority, but the stem cue is Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: This option overweights Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures, while the decisive cue is Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The safer answer is Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing because delaying infection or sepsis escalation can worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: The reasoning trap in Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, even though the higher-risk finding is Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone. It is less defensible because Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time.
